title: The Market Hall at Chipping Campden
summary: Built in 1627 by Sir Baptist Hicks, the Market Hall sheltered traders selling butter, cheese, and poultry. It remains one of the finest examples in the Cotswolds.
body: The Market Hall was built in 1627 by the wealthy merchant Sir Baptist Hicks as a shelter for market traders. Its open arches allowed goods — mainly butter, cheese, and poultry — to be sold in all weathers. Hicks, who made his fortune in the London silk trade, was a great benefactor to the town. The Hall was given to the National Trust in 1902 and remains one of the most photographed buildings in the Cotswolds. It stands at the start of the Cotswold Way, marking the beginning of many walkers' journeys south to Bath.

title: The Cotswold Wool Trade
summary: The golden stone buildings you see across the Cotswolds were funded by medieval wool wealth. Cotswold sheep produced the finest fleece in Europe.
body: Between the 13th and 16th centuries, the Cotswolds was one of the wealthiest regions in England. Cotswold Lion sheep produced fine, heavy fleeces that were exported across Europe. The 'wool churches' built with this wealth — at Chipping Campden, Northleach, and Cirencester — are among the finest parish churches in England. The honey-coloured limestone buildings that define the Cotswold landscape were largely built during this prosperous era. Even the name 'Chipping' comes from the Old English 'ceaping', meaning market.

title: Broadway Tower: A Folly with a View
summary: Built in 1798 for Lady Coventry, who wanted to know if a beacon on this hilltop could be seen from her home 22 miles away. It could.
body: Broadway Tower was designed by James Wyatt and built in 1798 for Lady Coventry of Croome Court in Worcestershire. She wanted to know whether a beacon lit on this hilltop — the second highest point in the Cotswolds — could be seen from her home 22 miles away. It could. The tower has since served as a retreat for William Morris and the Arts and Crafts movement, a Cold War nuclear bunker observation post, and is now a popular tourist attraction. On a clear day, 16 counties are visible from the top.

title: Broadway: Artists' Village
summary: In the 1880s, Broadway became a colony for American and British artists, drawn by the golden light and picturesque cottages.
body: In the 1880s, Broadway became an artists' colony when the American painter Francis Davis Millet settled there. He was soon joined by John Singer Sargent, Edwin Austin Abbey, and other leading artists of the day. The village's wide, tree-lined main street, honey-coloured buildings, and soft Cotswold light made it irresistible. Sargent painted his famous 'Carnation, Lily, Lily, Rose' in the garden of Farnham House here. The tradition continues today with the Gordon Russell Design Museum and numerous galleries.

title: Stanway's Gravity Fountain
summary: Stanway House has the tallest gravity-fed fountain in the world at 300 feet, powered entirely by the natural fall of water from the hillside above.
body: Stanway House, a magnificent Jacobean manor, is home to the tallest gravity-fed fountain in the world. Rising to 300 feet, the fountain is powered entirely by a natural water supply from the hills above — no pumps needed. The house has been in the same family, the Earls of Wemyss, since 1533. Its gatehouse is considered one of the finest in England, and the tithe barn dates from the 14th century. The cricket ground in the grounds is one of the most scenic in the country.

title: Winchcombe: Capital of Mercia
summary: In Saxon times, Winchcombe was the capital of its own shire and home to a great abbey. Today, traces of this ancient importance survive in its street plan.
body: Winchcombe was once the capital of the Anglo-Saxon kingdom of Winchcombeshire, with a powerful Benedictine abbey founded in 798 AD. The abbey was demolished during the Dissolution of the Monasteries under Henry VIII, but its stones were reused in buildings throughout the town. Nearby Sudeley Castle was the home and burial place of Katherine Parr, the last wife of Henry VIII. The town retains its medieval street plan and is celebrated for its walking festival each May.

title: Cleeve Common: Wild Hilltop
summary: Cleeve Common is the largest area of unimproved limestone grassland in Gloucestershire, home to rare orchids, skylarks, and ancient earthworks.
body: At 1,083 acres, Cleeve Common is the largest area of common land in Gloucestershire and a Site of Special Scientific Interest. The unimproved limestone grassland supports rare wildflowers including bee orchids, pyramidal orchids, and frog orchids. Skylarks nest on the open hillside and buzzards ride the thermals along the escarpment edge. The hilltop also contains Bronze Age ring cairns and an Iron Age cross-dyke. At 330 metres, this is the highest point on the entire Cotswold Way.

title: The Devil's Chimney
summary: This natural limestone pillar was left standing after quarrying. Legend says the devil rises from it on dark nights to sit on the hilltop.
body: The Devil's Chimney is a limestone pillar standing on Leckhampton Hill, created as a remnant of the extensive quarrying that took place from the 18th to early 20th century. The quarries supplied stone for much of Regency Cheltenham below. Local legend says the devil emerges from the chimney on dark nights to sit on the hilltop. In reality, the pillar has been reinforced with concrete and iron rods to prevent collapse. It has been climbed many times, most famously by a group of students in the 1980s who placed a traffic cone on top.

title: The 99 Yew Trees of Painswick
summary: Legend holds that there can never be more than 99 yew trees in Painswick churchyard — the devil always kills the hundredth.
body: St Mary's churchyard in Painswick is famous for its 99 clipped yew trees, many of which were planted in the 18th century. Local legend insists that there can never be 100 trees — whenever a hundredth is planted, the devil kills it. The reality is less dramatic: the count varies depending on how you define a separate tree. The churchyard also contains remarkable 17th and 18th-century table tombs carved by local masons, considered among the finest in England. The annual Clipping Ceremony (from 'ycleping', meaning embracing) sees the church encircled by parishioners holding hands.

title: Standish Wood: Ancient Woodland
summary: This ancient woodland has been continuously wooded since at least the medieval period. In spring, the forest floor erupts in a carpet of bluebells.
body: Standish Wood is classified as ancient semi-natural woodland, meaning it has been continuously wooded since at least 1600 AD and likely much longer. Dominated by beech trees, it sits on the Cotswold escarpment with dramatic views west. In late April and May, the woodland floor is carpeted with bluebells — one of the finest displays in Gloucestershire. The wood is also home to fallow deer, badgers, and a variety of woodland birds including nuthatches, treecreepers, and great spotted woodpeckers.

title: Nympsfield Long Barrow
summary: Near Coaley Peak lies Nympsfield Long Barrow, a Neolithic burial chamber dating from around 2800 BC — older than Stonehenge.
body: Just a short walk from Coaley Peak viewpoint lies Nympsfield Long Barrow, a Neolithic chambered tomb dating from around 2800 BC. When excavated, it contained the remains of over 20 individuals along with pottery and animal bones. The tomb was originally covered by a mound of earth and stone nearly 30 metres long. Today the stone chambers are exposed and freely accessible. Nearby Hetty Pegler's Tump, another long barrow, can also be explored with a torch. These monuments predate Stonehenge and the Egyptian pyramids.

title: Bath Abbey: Journey's End
summary: Bath Abbey has stood on this site since the 7th century. The current building, begun in 1499, is the last great medieval church built in England.
body: Bath Abbey, where many Cotswold Way walkers celebrate completing their 102-mile journey, has been a place of worship since the 7th century. The present building was begun in 1499 by Bishop Oliver King, who had a dream of angels ascending and descending a ladder to heaven — depicted on the west front. It is the last great medieval church built in England. The Abbey sits above the ancient Roman Baths, the only naturally occurring hot springs in the British Isles. The waters rise at 46°C from a depth of over 2,700 metres.

title: The Cotswold Lion Sheep
summary: The 'Cotswold Lion' is a rare breed of large, shaggy-fleeced sheep that once made the Cotswolds the richest region in medieval England.
body: The Cotswold Lion is a distinctive breed of sheep with long, curly fleeces that can grow to 15 inches. In the Middle Ages, their high-quality wool was so valuable it was known as the 'Golden Fleece' and was exported across Europe. The breed nearly died out in the 20th century but has been revived by dedicated farmers. You may still spot these large, gentle sheep with their characteristic fringes in fields along the Cotswold Way. The breed's historical importance is why a sheep appears on the coat of arms of the Lord Chancellor, who traditionally sits on a woolsack in the House of Lords.

title: Peregrine Falcons at Broadway Tower
summary: Peregrine falcons nest on Broadway Tower's stonework, diving at speeds over 200mph to hunt pigeons across the hilltop.
body: Broadway Tower is home to nesting peregrine falcons, the fastest animals on Earth. During their hunting stoop (dive), they can reach speeds exceeding 200mph. The falcons were driven to near-extinction in Britain by pesticide use in the 1960s but have made a remarkable recovery. The tower's elevated position and abundant prey (mainly pigeons and starlings) make it an ideal nesting site. Birdwatchers gather at the tower in spring to watch the adults bringing food to their chicks. If you look up as you pass, you may see one circling overhead.

title: The Golden Stone: Cotswold Limestone
summary: The honey-coloured buildings of the Cotswolds are built from Great Oolite limestone, formed 170 million years ago on a tropical seabed.
body: The distinctive golden stone of the Cotswolds is Great Oolite limestone, formed around 170 million years ago during the Jurassic period. At that time, this area lay beneath a warm, shallow sea near the equator. The limestone is made up of tiny spherical grains called ooliths (from the Greek for 'egg stone'), each formed as layers of calcium carbonate built up around a grain of sand. The stone varies in colour from pale cream in the north (around Broadway) to deeper honey and amber tones in the south (near Bath). This gradual colour change is visible as you walk the Cotswold Way.

title: The Ghostly Monks of Stanway
summary: Local legend tells of hooded monks seen walking through Stanway's grounds at dusk, remnants of the medieval Tewkesbury Abbey estate.
body: Stanway House was originally part of the estate of Tewkesbury Abbey, and monks are said to have farmed this land for centuries before the Dissolution. Locals report seeing hooded figures walking silently through the grounds at dusk, particularly near the tithe barn and the old gatehouse. The barn itself dates from the 14th century and would have stored the abbey's share of the local harvest. Whether the sightings are tricks of the fading Cotswold light or something more, the atmosphere at Stanway in the evening is undeniably atmospheric.

title: Katherine Parr at Sudeley Castle
summary: Sudeley Castle near Winchcombe was the final home of Katherine Parr, the last wife of Henry VIII, who is buried in the chapel.
body: After Henry VIII died in 1547, Katherine Parr married Thomas Seymour and moved to Sudeley Castle. She died there in 1548, just days after giving birth to her daughter Mary. She was buried in the castle chapel, but her tomb was lost for centuries after the castle was damaged in the Civil War. In 1782, her coffin was accidentally rediscovered by a local man. The castle has been beautifully restored and Katherine's tomb can still be visited in St Mary's Church within the grounds, making it the only private dwelling in England where a queen is buried.

title: Walking the Edge: The Cotswold Escarpment
summary: The dramatic west-facing escarpment you walk along was created by millions of years of erosion, exposing the limestone beneath.
body: The Cotswold escarpment is the dramatic western edge of the Cotswold Hills, running roughly north-south for over 50 miles. It was formed as rivers and ice eroded the softer clay beneath the harder limestone cap, creating the steep west-facing slopes that give the Cotswold Way its most spectacular views. The escarpment rises to over 300 metres at Cleeve Hill and offers views across the Severn Vale to the Forest of Dean, the Malvern Hills, and on clear days, the mountains of Wales. Much of the Cotswold Way follows this edge, which is why the views are consistently to the west.

title: The Quarrymen of Leckhampton
summary: Leckhampton Hill was heavily quarried for 200 years, providing the stone that built Regency Cheltenham's elegant terraces.
body: From the late 1700s to the early 1900s, Leckhampton Hill was the main source of building stone for the rapidly growing spa town of Cheltenham below. The quarrymen cut deep into the hillside, leaving dramatic cliff faces that are now popular with rock climbers. An inclined tramway once carried stone blocks down to the town. Working conditions were dangerous — several quarrymen were killed in rockfalls. The Devil's Chimney pillar was left standing as the quarry was cut around it, either deliberately as a landmark or simply because the stone was too fractured to use.

title: Painswick Rococo Garden
summary: Hidden behind the village is England's only surviving complete Rococo garden, a whimsical 18th-century landscape of paths, follies, and snowdrops.
body: The Painswick Rococo Garden is a unique survivor from the 1740s, a period when garden design was moving away from formal symmetry towards more playful, naturalistic styles. The garden was created for Benjamin Hyett and features winding paths, Gothic follies, a maze, and dramatic views down a steep coombe. It fell into neglect for over a century before being rediscovered and restored from the 1980s using a painting by Thomas Robins as the guide. In February, the garden is famous for its display of over a million snowdrops, drawing visitors from across the country.

title: Bluebell Season in the Woods
summary: In late April and May, the ancient beech woods along the escarpment erupt in a carpet of native bluebells — a sight unique to Britain.
body: Britain is home to around half the world's population of native bluebells, and the ancient woodlands along the Cotswold Way are among the best places to see them. The display peaks in late April to mid-May, when the forest floor turns a vivid violet-blue. Native bluebells (Hyacinthoides non-scripta) have narrow, tubular bells that droop to one side and a sweet scent. They thrive in ancient woodland because they flower before the tree canopy closes, using the brief window of spring light. Standish Wood, Buckholt Wood, and the beech hangers above Painswick are particularly spectacular.

title: Hetty Pegler's Tump: A Name from the Past
summary: This Neolithic burial chamber near Coaley Peak is named after a 17th-century landowner — but the tomb itself is nearly 5,000 years old.
body: Hetty Pegler's Tump (formally Uley Long Barrow) is a Neolithic chambered tomb dating from around 3000 BC. It takes its popular name from Hester Pegler, who owned the surrounding land in the 1600s. The mound is 37 metres long and still has its original dry-stone walled entrance passage and burial chambers. When excavated, remains of at least 28 individuals were found inside. Unlike nearby Nympsfield Long Barrow, Hetty Pegler's Tump retains its earth covering and can be entered with a torch — a genuinely atmospheric experience. A key is available from the nearby farm.

title: Dursley and the Cloth Trade
summary: Dursley was once a thriving centre of the Cotswold cloth trade. The town's prosperity built the fine church of St James.
body: From the 14th to the 18th century, Dursley was an important centre of the Cotswold cloth trade, processing the wool from the sheep that grazed the surrounding hills. The town's wealth funded the construction of St James the Great church, with its distinctive Gothic tower. The cloth mills were powered by the streams running off the escarpment. As the Industrial Revolution moved textile production to the north of England, Dursley's cloth trade declined. The Lister family later brought engineering industry to the town — R.A. Lister & Company manufactured diesel engines and agricultural equipment here for over a century.

title: The Dursley Lantern
summary: A local legend tells of a mysterious light seen floating above the town on misty nights, said to be the ghost of a lost traveller.
body: The Dursley Lantern is a piece of local folklore describing a ghostly light that appears above the town on foggy autumn nights. The story goes that a medieval traveller, lost in the mist on the escarpment above Dursley, lit a lantern to find his way but walked over the cliff edge. His light is said to still be seen on misty nights, floating along the hilltop. While the phenomenon is likely caused by atmospheric conditions and distant lights refracting in the fog, the story has been told in Dursley pubs for generations. Some say if you see it, you should not follow.

title: The Electric Picture House
summary: Wotton-under-Edge is home to one of the oldest purpose-built cinemas in the world, still showing films since 1913.
body: The Electric Picture House in Wotton-under-Edge opened in 1913 and is believed to be one of the oldest purpose-built cinemas still in continuous operation anywhere in the world. The building was designed specifically as a cinema (rather than converted from a theatre or hall) and retains much of its original character. It seats just 100 people and is run entirely by volunteers from the community. The cinema shows a mix of new releases, classic films, and live event screenings. It is a remarkable survival in an age of multiplexes and streaming.

title: Edward Jenner: Pioneer of Vaccination
summary: The world's first vaccine was developed just miles from here by Edward Jenner, who grew up in the countryside around Berkeley.
body: Edward Jenner, born in Berkeley in 1749, developed the world's first successful vaccine. Working as a country doctor, he noticed that milkmaids who had caught cowpox seemed immune to smallpox. In 1796, he tested his theory by inoculating a boy with cowpox material and later exposing him to smallpox — the boy was immune. Jenner's discovery led to the eventual eradication of smallpox in 1980, saving countless millions of lives. His home in Berkeley is now a museum. The rolling countryside you walk through between Wotton and Dursley would have been familiar to Jenner on his rounds.

title: The Battle of Nibley Green
summary: In 1470, the last private battle in England was fought near here between two feuding noble families, the Talbots and the Berkeleys.
body: On 20 March 1470, Lord Lisle and Viscount Berkeley settled their long-running land dispute the medieval way — with a pitched battle. Around 1,000 men fought at Nibley Green, between Wotton-under-Edge and North Nibley. Lord Lisle was killed, shot through the face with an arrow, and his body was stripped and left on the field. The battle is considered the last private battle fought in England. A monument on the hillside above North Nibley, the Tyndale Monument, commemorates not the battle but William Tyndale, who translated the Bible into English and is believed to have been born nearby.

title: Old Sodbury Iron Age Hillfort
summary: The earthwork banks around Old Sodbury church are the remains of an Iron Age hillfort, occupied for over a thousand years.
body: The ridge at Old Sodbury has been a defensive position since at least the Iron Age, around 500 BC. The hillfort's earth ramparts and ditches enclosed a large area that would have housed a community and their livestock. The site was strategically important, controlling the route along the escarpment and the crossing of the Bristol Avon below. The Norman church of St John the Baptist was later built within the fort's defences, reusing the ancient earthworks. From the churchyard, you can still trace the line of the ramparts and appreciate why this hilltop was chosen — the views in every direction are commanding.

title: The Battle of Lansdown: Civil War on the Ridge
summary: In 1643, one of the bloodiest battles of the English Civil War was fought on the ridge above Cold Ashton.
body: On 5 July 1643, Royalist forces under Sir Ralph Hopton attacked the Parliamentary army of Sir William Waller on Lansdown Hill, just north of Cold Ashton. The battle was fierce and indecisive — both sides suffered heavy casualties in the steep terrain. Sir Bevil Grenville, one of the Royalist commanders, was killed leading a charge up the hill. A monument to Grenville still stands on the battlefield. The Royalists held the hill at nightfall but were too weakened to press their advantage. You pass near the battlefield site as you walk the final ridge towards Bath.

title: The Ghost of Hanging Hill
summary: Hanging Hill above Cold Ashton is said to be haunted by the ghost of a woman in white, seen at dawn walking towards the cliff edge.
body: Hanging Hill, which the Cotswold Way crosses between Cold Ashton and Bath, takes its ominous name from its steep, overhanging cliff. Local stories tell of a woman in white seen walking the hilltop path at dawn, always moving towards the cliff edge before vanishing. The identity of the ghost varies with the telling — some say she was a jilted bride, others a Civil War widow searching for her husband after the Battle of Lansdown. The hilltop is genuinely atmospheric in early morning mist, and the views from the edge are spectacular but vertiginous. The name 'Hanging Hill' likely comes from the Old English for 'steep slope' rather than anything more sinister.

title: The Hot Springs: 10,000 Years of Bathing
summary: Bath's hot springs have been used for over 10,000 years. The water falls as rain on the Mendip Hills and rises at 46°C after travelling 2km underground.
body: The thermal springs at Bath are the only naturally occurring hot springs in the British Isles. Rainwater falling on the nearby Mendip Hills sinks deep underground, where geothermal energy heats it to between 64°C and 96°C. It then rises through faults in the limestone to emerge at the surface at around 46°C. The springs produce over a million litres of water per day. Archaeological evidence shows people have been bathing here since at least 8000 BC. The Romans built an elaborate bathing complex around the springs in the 1st century AD, and the Georgian city we see today was built on the same tradition of 'taking the waters'.
